event: Stone Age hunter-gatherers inhabit the savannas and forests of what is now Benin, leaving Acheulean and later Middle Stone Age tools.
Neolithic communities begin cultivating crops such as yams and practicing settled agriculture across the region.
Iron-working spreads through the region, enabling more productive farming and the growth of larger settlements.
Speakers of Gbe languages, including the Aja, Fon, and Ewe peoples, settle the southern coastal and forest zones.
The Aja people establish the kingdom of Allada (Great Ardra) in the south, a center of regional power and trade.
Portuguese navigators reach the coast, opening European contact along what becomes known as the Slave Coast.
The kingdom of Dahomey emerges among the Fon people on the Abomey plateau under early rulers of the line of Houegbadja.
The coastal kingdom of Hogbonu (Porto-Novo) is founded, becoming an important Atlantic trading port.
Houegbadja consolidates the kingdom of Dahomey around its capital at Abomey.
King Agaja ascends the Dahomey throne and begins aggressive expansion toward the coast.
Dahomey under Agaja conquers the kingdom of Allada, expanding its power southward.
Dahomey conquers the port of Ouidah (Whydah), gaining direct access to the Atlantic slave trade.
Dahomey becomes a tributary state of the powerful Oyo Empire of the Yoruba to the east.
King Ghezo seizes the Dahomey throne and ends tributary subjugation to Oyo, restoring the kingdom's independence.
France signs a commercial treaty with Dahomey, deepening European involvement on the coast.
King Glele continues Dahomey's expansion and its reliance on the elite female warrior corps known as the Dahomey Amazons.
France establishes a protectorate over Porto-Novo, beginning formal colonial encroachment.
King Behanzin ascends the throne of Dahomey and resists growing French demands.
The First Franco-Dahomean War erupts as France moves to subjugate the kingdom.
France defeats King Behanzin, exiles him, and annexes Dahomey as a colony.
Dahomey is incorporated into the federation of French West Africa.
Dahomey becomes a French overseas territory with representation in the French parliament.
Dahomey becomes a self-governing republic within the French Community.
Dahomey gains full independence from France on 1 August, with Hubert Maga as first president.
Dahomey is admitted to the United Nations on 20 September.
A military coup led by Colonel Christophe Soglo overthrows President Maga amid political instability.
Repeated political crises lead to further military intervention and a period of unstable civilian-military rule.
Major Mathieu Kerekou seizes power in a military coup, ending years of revolving-door governments.
Kerekou declares Dahomey a Marxist-Leninist state aligned with socialist principles.
The country is renamed the People's Republic of Benin, after the historic Bight of Benin.
A mercenary force attempts an invasion of Cotonou but is repelled by Kerekou's government.
Facing economic collapse and protests, Kerekou abandons Marxism-Leninism as the state ideology.
A national conference of living forces establishes a transitional government and a multiparty democracy.
Nicephore Soglo defeats Kerekou in free elections, marking one of Africa's first peaceful transfers of power from a dictatorship.
Mathieu Kerekou returns to power by winning the presidential election, this time as a democrat.
Kerekou is re-elected president amid an opposition boycott of the runoff.
Political outsider Yayi Boni wins the presidency, succeeding Kerekou in a peaceful transition.
Yayi Boni is re-elected to a second term as president.
Businessman Patrice Talon wins the presidency, defeating the candidate backed by the outgoing government.
Legislative elections held without opposition participation spark protests and a contraction of political space.
Patrice Talon is re-elected to a second term in a contested vote marked by low opposition participation.
France returns 26 royal treasures looted from the Kingdom of Dahomey in 1892, a landmark in cultural restitution.
Jihadist attacks spill over from the Sahel into northern Benin, straining national security.
Benin continues efforts to develop tourism and museums around its Dahomey heritage while confronting northern insecurity.
